title: The M5 iPad Pro is NOT a MacBook! But yeah…it can replace one.
transcript: This M5 iPad Pro is not a MacBook. Is it super powerful and fast? Yes. Can you do all kinds of productivity on it, like creating PowerPoints or managing spreadsheets or typing up research papers? Yep. Can you do things on it that you can't do on a MacBook, like take handwritten notes or use cellular or get access to really good mobile games? Yes. Yes, you can. But all that being said, it's still not a MacBook and it never will be. Hey everyone, Tech Dad here and we have almost demanded that Apple turn the iPad into a MacBook like device and I am guilty of that for sure. I've wanted to do everything on my iPad since the thing came out in like 2010. And honestly, Apple is trying to deliver. They have given us iPad OS 26 this year. So now we have a lot of Mac-like features in the operating system. We've got windowing like a Mac. We can manage files very similar to a Mac. They're giving us accessories to turn our iPads into the Mac. They have the Magic Keyboard. You can connect to a secondary display. But the cold hard truth is the M5 iPad Pro is not a MacBook. But can it replace a MacBook? That is the question at hand. And the answer is, oh yeah. For so many people, the M5 iPad Pro is probably way more computing power than you're ever going to need or use. So, in this video, we have to break down what the M5 iPad Pro is and what it isn't. We have to talk about its purpose and how it's different from the MacBook. We need to talk about the tradeoffs. What are you getting with an iPad that you don't get with a MacBook? And vice versa. And finally, we will answer the question for you. Can you replace your MacBook? Just get the M5 iPad Pro and be good to go. So, without further ado, let's jump into it. All right. So, let's start by defining the M5 iPad Pro and what it is. So, the iPad is its own category of device. Steve Jobs made this very clear. It is not a laptop. It is not a big iPhone. Can't stand hearing that anymore. It's a tablet. It's its own thing. It's designed to be held in hand. It's designed to use touch controls first. Can you slap a keyboard and trackpad on this thing? Yes. Does it work as well as a MacBook? Uh, no. So, I use my keyboard and trackpad a lot, but that doesn't mean it's not buggy, and that certainly doesn't mean it's on par with a MacBook. Keyboards and trackpads were designed for computers first way back in the '90s. While the iPad wasn't designed for using keyboards and trackpads, it was designed to use through touch. And even though we have modified it, and Apple has changed it quite a bit over the years, it really is still a touch first device. And that's how it works the best. And I think where people get frustrated is they hook this thing to a keyboard and trackpad. They try to use it like a MacBook. It doesn't work exactly like a MacBook and then they want to take the thing and throw it in the garbage. And so if you want to save yourself some frustration, stop trying to believe that your iPad Pro is a MacBook. Start trying to use it as a tablet. Put it in your hands. Make a PowerPoint presentation with the touch controls and even use Apple Pencil because that is an incredible addition to the iPad Pro. So, Apple Pencil Pro gives you all kinds of haptic feedback. It gives you some precision when grabbing text. It has hover like a mouse, so you can hover over before you select something. So, there's a lot of power in the Apple Pencil that really makes the tablet functionality come alive, and it's something that you're not getting with a MacBook. Okay, techad, we recognize that the iPad Pro is a tablet, but what does that mean? What are you getting at? Well, to answer that, we need to talk about the tradeoffs. And I always like to use car analogies. So, let's say you look at a Ford F-150 pickup truck versus a Ford Mustang. You could say that they are both vehicles. They both fall into the same category of vehicles. They can both drive you to the grocery store, no problem. They can both go over 90 mph, no problem. But just because they have those similarities doesn't mean you can categorize them as the same thing. Trucks can tow thousands of pounds of equipment. They can haul humongous loads in their truck bed. They're really great for off-road conditions and snowy road conditions. The Mustang is not good at any of that. It offers a completely different experience and it has some things that it's better at. It has a much higher top speed. It's sleeker and is probably considered better looking because it's a sports car. It sounds different. The cab is different. And that's the way it is between a Mac and an iPad. They're really different categories of devices. and you get pluses and minuses with each device. So, with the iPad Pro, you get functionality that you're not going to get with the MacBook. You get that awesome, ridiculously thin form factor. You get ultra portability. You get the option of cellular that MacBooks don't have. You get to take handwritten notes that the MacBook just can't do. But on the other side, with Mac, you get all kinds of things the iPad can't do. You get a better mouse and keyboard experience. You get a more powerful operating system that was designed under the hood to handle more work, more complicated software, more tasks at a time. You get a more advanced web browsing experience because the desktop browser is just more advanced. You can connect to more accessories like multiple monitors. The iPad only supports one monitor. So, I like to think of the MacBook as a workhorse or as an F-150 pickup. And I like to think of the iPad Pro as that sports car. You can definitely get around with this thing, but it's also just really gorgeous to look at and use, and it just gives those other factors that I've listed that just give it advantages over a MacBook. So, the question is, can this M5 iPad Pro replace your MacBook? Well, that's kind of like asking, can a car replace a truck? And the answer is yes for some people. Some people buy a truck and never do anything with it. They don't haul anything or tow anything. They just drive it around. Well, if that's the case, they could probably drive a Mustang around just as well as they could their truck. But sometimes you need to tow a 10,000lb camper and the Mustang's not going to do that. So sometimes you can't get the car or if your life permits it, just get both. Have your sports car for driving around for fun and then have your truck for the work. And that's the way it is with Mac and iPad. You can do all kinds of great work on an iPad Pro, including productivity, video editing, graphic design. But if you have some specific softwares or a heavy workload, just get the Mac. Me myself, I use an iPad Pro 95% of the time, but I have a Mac Mini just for those rare occasions where I need a desktop application that has the extra features or I need a desktop browser or I want to play some retro video games like World of Warcraft Classic. iPad's not going to do that. So, if you're considering purchasing or if you're looking into the iPad, just keep those considerations in mind. Let me know what you think of this whole can an iPad replace a MacBook debate. You can leave a comment below. That's all I got for you. If you like this sort of content, please like and subscribe.

title: Intel vs Apple Silicon – Ya Basta de Esta HUMILLACIÓN…
transcript: There was a time when MacBooks had Intel chips, because Intel processors provided the muscle and Apple only designed the casing.  As the 2010s progressed, that alliance began to creak.  The x86 chips became energy-inefficient and improvements came in dribs and drabs. Meanwhile, Apple was manufacturing its own A-series chips for iPhones based on ARM architecture and began to wonder why not use that same recipe.  for MacBooks.  In 2020, Apple announced its split with Intel and introduced its M1 chip, the first Apple Silicon chip.  This processor marked the leap from the complex X86 ARM architecture and brought together CPU, GPU, memory and neural engine into a single system-on-a- chip with unified memory.  That decision allowed Apple to control its schedule, improve hardware and software integration, and differentiate its products.  x86 apps continue to work on new MacBooks thanks to Rosetta 2, which translates instructions on the fly.  In 2024, the Apple Silicon M4 arrived, manufactured in 3 nanometers with up to 10 cores, four for performance and six for efficiency, a 10- core GPU with ray tracing and a 16-core Eural Engine capable of 38 trillion operations per second.  The efficiency gains allowed the MacBook to offer almost 20 hours of battery life, consolidating Apple's advantage.  The key to Apple Silicon is unified memory.  CPU, GPU, Neural Engine share the same, let's say, data pool and do not need to copy information between chips.  This reduces latency and saves a lot of energy.  [music] It also eliminates dependencies on third parties, as Apple controls the design and relies on TSMC only for manufacturing and the end result.  It doesn't depend on anyone else.  The transition coincided with a period of stagnation in Intel's improvements and delays in the manufacturing of its nodes.  Apple's decision was as practical as it was strategic. For many users it was like going from driving a noisy diesel car to a modern, quiet electric vehicle. The route is the same, but the experience is completely different. During the first couple of years of the transition, MacBooks with M1 processors and MacBooks with Intel processors coexisted.  Many professionals were cautious, because what would happen to their old applications?  Would they be as fast as they promised?  Apple responded with real- world experiences.  The MacBook Air with M5 broke battery life records and outperformed many Intel Core i7 processors of that era.  And since then, each generation—M2, M3, M4, M5—has added more efficient cores, new features, more power, and so on.  The developer community also jumped on the universal compilation bandwagon, and [music] compatibility with iPhone and iPad apps became just a bonus.  And finally, recently, in October 2025, Apple introduced the M5 chip, an evolution focused on AI.  This type incorporates a 10-core CPU, a 10-core GPU with neural acceleration on each core, which quadruples the power and increases graphics performance by up to 30% compared to the M4. [music] Apple claims the multithreaded CPU is 20% faster and that the new 16-core Neural Engine benefits from 153GB of memory bandwidth, which is 30% more than [music] before.  As a result, the latest 14-inch MacBook Pro M5 offers 24 hours of all-day battery life and graphics that are 1.6 times faster.  Editing 8K videos, running a language mode feels as fast as switching tabs in Safari.  If we're talking about power, well, it's not all about Apple Silicon chips [music]. Sure, the M-family chips are very efficient and fast, but what good is having a faster processor if the keyboard doesn't keep up?  That's where the nu fuo V2 iOS series comes in.  It's a mechanical keyboard that redefines what it means to enjoy typing.  It's almost the physical equivalent of an M5 chip; it's precise, elegant, and powerful enough to be felt in every key.  Its triple-mode system connects via USBC, Bluetooth, and also has a 2.4 GHz connection, allowing you to switch between Mac, iPad, or PC without latency, and its 4000 mAh battery can last up to 100 hours of music playback.  It's crazy when you compare it to the battery life of even a MacBook, it lasts 24 hours, right?  What really stands out are the new switches, as it has Red Max, Brown Max and Blush Max designed by Nu to offer three experiences: linear, tactile or silent.  All with refined sound and an incredibly satisfying response.  I have a silent version, so you literally don't hear the sound of the keys at all.  It is available in 65, 75 and 96% [music] formats and in Obsidian Black, Ionic White and Sakura Fish colors.  Well, it fits into practically any setup.  I have the 75% version, that is, without the numbers here on the right and in white, so you can see how it works in real use.  [music] And if you're up for trying it, you get a 50% discount with the code J15.  The link, as always, is in the description, and we'll return to Intel.  How does all that power compare to what Intel promises us?  Because of course they're still developing their own processors, right?  Intel has responded with its Lunar Lake generation, designed for artificial intelligence in PCs. This chip reduces system power consumption by 40% and triples AI capabilities compared to its predecessor, thanks to an NPU with up to 48 cores and a new XE2 GPU.  [music] It's manufactured by TSMC, of ​​course, and uses high-performance Lion Cove cores and Skyol efficiency cores rearranged with 3D FO technology. Intel wants to demonstrate that x86 laptops can be just as efficient as ARM-based ones.  The counterattack will come with Panner Lake, scheduled for 2026, built on the 18 process. Intel's name for the 2 nann process is for up to 16 cores and promises more than 50% improvement in CPU and GPU performance.  [music] It will also incorporate a balanced XPU architecture with up to 180 power tops per day.  This is the first time Intel has integrated CPU and GP into a single consumer-level package.  The company is confident that Panther Lake will narrow its gap with Apple.  But what does that matter to the user?  Which one is better now?  Well, Intel or Apple Silicon.  Look, for creators, for developers, the M5 really does offer tangible advantages. Its GPU with Euro Accelerators, neural accelerators, accelerates tools, I don't know, like LM Studio, Draw Things.  The enormous unified memory also allows loading large models without the need for external cards.  [music] And frameworks like Metal 4 take advantage of the GPU and Neural Engine effortlessly from the developer.  Furthermore, battery performance barely drops; you can render a 3D scene or train a neural network on the train without running out of power.  Intel's Lunar Lake improves efficiency in Windows laptops and offers a 48-core NPU for other functions. However, actual battery life is usually around 10 hours or so, and many apps are not yet optimized for the NPU, so they rely on the CPU or GPU. for gaming and certain professional applications.  Intel processors still have an advantage due to the extensive software library for x86 and the ability to upgrade components.  The M-series Macs are very closed machines.  You need to decide on the memory and storage configuration when purchasing.  In other words, you can't buy a MacBook with, for example, 512 GB of memory and then install an additional 2 terabyte SSD.  No, you have to choose and pay for storage directly in the store.  With laptops that have Intel chips, you can do it and save yourself quite a bit of money.  And you might ask, the title speaks of humiliation in a joking tone, where is all that humiliation?  Don't misunderstand, it's not that Intel is finished.  The company continues to lead in servers, [music] in workstations, in many desktop PCs, but in thin laptops, in very efficient laptops.  Apple clearly has the advantage.  The change began with the iPhone, which demonstrated the potential of ARM, and culminated with the M1 and its successors, the M2, etc.  The situation is about as absurd as seeing a sumo wrestler competing in a marathon race. Intel dominates in brute force, they are very powerful, but Apple wins in lightness, resistance, efficiency, which is basically what we look for in our daily lives for work, for leisure, for gaming, for everything simply.  Furthermore, the fight is not only Apple against Intel, as AMD, having launched Snapdragon XLT, is preparing the AI300 series. Both are ARM chips designed for Windows laptops, but we might talk about those next time. Right now, at the end of 2025, Apple Silicon dominates the premium laptop segment due to efficiency, battery life, and integration.  The latest M5 makes the MacBook an ideal machine for creative work, while Intel seeks to regain ground with its Lunar Lake and Panther Lake.  Competition is actually good, because the humiliation that took place in 2020 forces Intel to innovate and ensure that the race for perfection continues to accelerate.  So, which one is better?  Intel or Apple Silicon.  Well, if you're looking for the best performance per watt and you live in the Apple M5 ecosystem, it 's purely the king [of music] today. If you need to play a lot of games or build your own PC from scratch, Intel can still offer you something.  I hope this was helpful, and see you next time. Yeah.

title: Samsung Galaxy G Fold Officially Revealed - First Look!
transcript: So Samsung just went ahead and officially  showed off the Galaxy G Fold, or the Z TriFold,   and here’s everything you need to know. What's up, guys Sal here, so in an event   in South Korea, Samsung gave a sneak peek of the  Galaxy Z TriFold to a select group of people. It   wasn’t a full public reveal, though... the phone  was showcased behind glass, so no one actually   got to touch it. The full launch is expected  to happen in the next few days, most likely on   October 31st. But even with that limited showing,  we’ve already got some solid footage and images   that give us our best look yet at this thing. As you can see, it folds in a G-shape design   exactly like the leaks and firmware animations  hinted at. The cover display sits in the middle,   the triple camera setup is on the left panel,  and the right panel is just an empty section.   The cover screen measures 6.5 inches, and when  fully opened, it unfolds into a 10-inch tablet.   Now, one of the first things you’ll notice  right away is the bezels, they’re definitely   not small. In fact, they look noticeably  thicker than what we saw on the Z Fold 7,   both on the cover display and the main screen.  There's a punch hole camera on the inner screen,   same as the Fold 7, and on the back, the camera  housing also looks identical to the fold 7 and   the specs are expected to stay the same too. Now  what's interesting is that recently Samsung’s   leaked firmware hinted at 100x zoom on this  phone, while the Fold 7 only goes up to 30x. That started speculation that Samsung might  have added a 5x periscope zoom lens like the   one on the S25 Ultra. But from what we can see  here, the third lens looks very similar to the   one on the Z Fold 7, not the Ultra. The Ultra’s  periscope lens is noticeably larger, so my guess   is the Z TriFold still uses a 3x telephoto lens.  Is that 100% confirmed? nope, since no one could   go hands-on with the device, so we’ll have to  wait for the full reveal to know for sure. As for the thickness, it’s surprisingly  reasonable. When folded, it looks about   as thick as an index finger, which probably puts  it in the 12 to 13mm range, roughly the same as   the Z Fold 6. That’s pretty impressive for a  phone with three separate panels. Each panel   looks super thin too — around 4mm by the  looks of it, judging from the size of the   volume buttons and fingerprint sensor. I was  honestly expecting this thing to be a brick,   but it seems Samsung actually managed to keep  it sleek, at least by tri-fold standards. Also, if you look closely, the frame isn’t  completely flat. Around the fingerprint   scanner and volume buttons, it slightly curves  inward. And that’s actually a smart move.   It fixes one of the biggest design annoyances  with the Z Fold 7. Since that phone was so thin,   it could be tricky to open, especially in the  morning when your hands are dry or sleepy. I’ve   even dropped it a couple of times because  of that. But this new inward curve gives   you a subtle notch to slide your thumb into and  open the phone more easily. It’s a small tweak,   but it makes a huge difference in daily use. Now, Samsung has confirmed that they are planning   to bring this trifold to users within this year.  But the availability story is where things get   confusing. According to Evan Blass, it might  be limited to just five markets, South Korea,   greater China, and the UAE among them, but no US  release, at least initially. That’s disappointing,   but Samsung’s comment gives a bit of hope.  If they weren’t planning a wider release,   they probably wouldn’t have said it’s  coming “to users within this year.” So, reading between the lines, Samsung might  launch it in select markets first and then expand   to others, possibly including the US, later this  year. Production numbers are rumored to be between   50,000 and 200,000 units, which makes sense given  the price and how experimental this thing is. Speaking of price, yeah, it’s not cheap.  The Korea Herald reports it’ll cost around   $2,800 in South Korea, about $1,000 more  than the Z Fold 7. That’s a huge jump,   but to be fair, this is a brand-new form factor.  Hopefully, once Samsung scales up production,   they can bring the cost closer to $2,200 —  that would make it a lot more appealing. Spec-wise, the Snapdragon 8 Elite chip is expected  under the hood, not the newer 8 Elite Gen 5. RAM   goes up to 16GB, storage up to 1TB, and while the  camera setup might stay the same as the Fold 7,   we still need clarity on that 100x zoom situation.  The battery capacity hasn’t been confirmed either,   but if I had to guess, I’d say around 5500mAh. Samsung is expected to fully unveil the phone   on October 31st, so we won’t have to wait  long to get all the details. And by the way,   there’s actually one more key feature of  this Z TriFold that might make this a real   tablet replacement. Click here to watch  that video, and I’ll see you there.
